text: in this video we describe our tip and trick for sparing the uterine cavity in case of a big type two five ten centimeter amaioma in case of fertility surgery. The risk during this surgery is to have late complication as a big synesia and we will show how to reduce the risk of synesia and how to do a safe laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter and opens the lateral space to access the pararectal fossa. The uterine artery is skeletonized for safe clip placement, with the ureter visible below the grasper. The tissue medial to the uterine artery is removed, revealing the enlarged ureter due to myoma compression.

The surgeon performs the same procedure on the right side, opening the peritoneum lateral to the infundibulopelvic ligament. The gas inflates the avascular retroperitoneal space, revealing the internal iliac artery. Fatty tissue is removed from around the internal iliac artery, and dissection of the pararectal fossa is initiated using traction and counter-traction. The uterine artery, identified as the first medial branch of the anterior iliac artery, is clipped safely at its origin.

The surgeon begins by opening the serosa surrounding the large 10-centimeter type 2-5 myoma, which has intracavitary repercussions. After clipping both uterine arteries, the myometrial capsule is progressively opened to facilitate the removal of the myoma.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the myometrial capsule, demonstrating a bloodless procedure. The next step involves using a ten millimeter forceps to apply traction on the myoma and increase the hystereotomy for the removal of the ten centimeter myoma.

The surgeon performs a seven-centimeter hystereotomy to facilitate the extraction of the myoma. The operative field remains dry due to effective myoma traction. The assistant uses a ten-millimeter forceps placed laterally to cut the surrounding tissue of the myoma. The assistant also retracts the normal serosa while employing the hydro dissection technique, using an aspirator.

The surgeon carefully treats the intracavitary part of the myoma, ensuring to spare the surrounding endometrium. Water is applied to the residual endometrium to reduce heat from the ultrasonic scalpel, visibly preserving the endometrial tissue. The dissecting plane is identified, allowing for the preservation of both the endometrium and the myometrial capsule.

The surgeon uses traction to remove the intracavitary part of the myoma, ensuring that the endometrium and surrounding myometrial tissue remain untouched. The myoma is nearly extracted from its bed, and the procedure is performed carefully to preserve the uterine cavity.

The surgeon identifies and respects normal uterine tissue while minimizing the use of harmonic energy. The uterus is completely removed from its bed, maintaining a bloodless surgical field without the need for electrical hemostasis.

The surgeon closes the myoma bed using stitches with a large 14-millimeter needle, placing sutures two centimeters outside the uterine scar. The healthy myometrium is indented into the myoma bed, and the knot is secured using intracorporeal knotting technique.

The surgeon locks the knot using intracorporeal knotting technique, ensuring the knot provides significant hemostasis. The knot is placed two centimeters away from the myoma edges, and a reverse knot is tied, taking a large bite of healthy tissue to invaginate it into the myoma bed.

The assistants invaginate healthy tissue into the myoma bed, demonstrating effective anatomical reconstruction. The surgeon uses V Quill sutures to tie knots and closes the myoma bed with stitches placed every centimeter, ensuring complete restoration of normal anatomy while avoiding stitches near the fallopian tube.

The surgeon performs a double flat knot technique to invaginate the residual healthy myometrium into the myoma bed, with the assistants locking the knot. The final knot is placed laterally, far from the myometrial scar, ensuring progressive closure and invagination of the tissue.

The surgeon performs morcellation using a 15mm electromechanical morcellator positioned in the left iliac fossa. The morcellator operates at 700 volts per minute, and the procedure is conducted under direct and continuous visual guidance, showcasing the final aspect of the surgery.
